Zustand selector granularity is the #1 performance lever in this architecture. The updateProject helper creates new project objects on every mutation, but sub-properties retain their references. params.query: Maximum update depth exceeded infinite loop re-render Zustand

params.description: Fixed "Maximum update depth exceeded" infinite render loop caused by 9 Zustand selectors returning non-primitive values (full Project objects, conversation objects, Maps, arrays) from the projects store. During Claude streaming, appendToLastAssistantText fires 50+ times/sec, each creating a new Map via updateProject(). Components subscribing to s.projects.get(id) got a new object reference on every update, causing cascading re-renders that exceeded React's update depth limit.

Zustand + Map store pattern: The combination of Map-based state and spread-based updates means EVERY selector that returns .get() output will fire on EVERY store update, not just updates to the key you're interested in. This is because updateProject creates a new Map AND a new project object via spread on every call. The only safe patterns are: (1) return primitives from selectors, (2) use useShallow for arrays/objects you genuinely need, (3) use getState() for imperative reads in event handlers/effects.

Fixed "Maximum update depth exceeded" infinite loop in FileTree.tsx. The auto-load effect used `!hasChildren` (children.length > 0) as a guard, but empty directories always have hasChildren=false even after loading. This caused: loadDir → sets empty array → hasChildren still false → isLoading false → effect re-fires → loadDir again → infinite loop. Fix: added `isDirLoaded` selector checking `dirContents.has(entry.path)` which returns true even for empty directories.

Comprehensive audit of "Maximum update depth exceeded" in Pane. Found 3 root causes: (1) Empty directory infinite loop — useEffect guard used `!hasChildren` (children.length > 0) but empty dirs always have 0 children, causing loadDir to fire infinitely. Fixed with `isDirLoaded` (dirContents.has()). (2) React hooks ordering violation — isDirLoaded hook and useEffect were placed after `if (!activeProjectId) return null` conditional return in FileTreeNode. Moved all hooks before the early return. (3) Module-level `settingsLoaded` flag wasn't reset on cleanup, meaning after error boundary recovery the save subscription would immediately start saving empty store state. Added `settingsLoaded = false` to cleanup.

Found and fixed the ACTUAL root cause of 'Maximum update depth exceeded'. ProjectList.tsx used useShallow with a selector that created inline objects: s.projectOrder.map(id => ({ id, name: p.name, hasUnread: p.hasUnreadCompletion })). useShallow compares array elements with Object.is, but new object literals always fail Object.is — so getSnapshot always returns 'changed', causing useSyncExternalStore to loop infinitely. Fix: extracted each row into a ProjectRow component with individual primitive selectors (name: string, hasUnread: boolean, isActive: boolean).

useShallow on arrays of objects is almost always wrong for streaming data. During Claude streaming, every text delta creates a new messages array AND mutates the last message object. useShallow does O(n) comparison on every store update, which is worse than just subscribing to the array reference directly. For streaming scenarios: (1) subscribe to array reference directly, (2) memo individual list items, (3) use message count as dependency for derived data that only changes when messages are added/removed.

params.description: Fixed scroll performance at three layers: GPU compositor, CSS containment, and passive listeners. The previous session only addressed React render cycles which was the wrong layer entirely.

Scroll performance on high-DPI displays is a compositor problem, not a React problem. On a 3024x1964 ProMotion display, Electron defaults to software rasterization because Chromium GPU-blocklists many Mac GPUs. This means the CPU is compositing ~6 million pixels per frame. No amount of React.memo or useShallow optimization will fix that. The fix hierarchy: (1) GPU rasterization flags, (2) CSS content-visibility for native virtualization, (3) passive scroll listeners, (4) React render optimization is last resort.

Scroll jank in both chat and editor traced to custom ::-webkit-scrollbar CSS which disables macOS native overlay scrollbar GPU-accelerated compositing. Replaced with `scrollbar-width: thin; scrollbar-color` (standard CSS). Also added `will-change: transform` on all scroll containers (Conversation, Terminal, FileTree, Ace scroller) to promote them to GPU compositor layers for async scrolling.

Root cause of 20fps scroll jank: Electron 33 has a bug on macOS 26 Tahoe where a private _cornerMask API override in ElectronNSWindow forces WindowServer to treat window masks as dynamic, causing persistent high GPU load. Fixed in electron/electron#48376, backported to Electron 36+. Upgraded Pane from Electron 33.4.11 to 40.6.0 (Chromium 144).

params.goal: Research scroll momentum settling stutter in Electron on macOS ProMotion 120Hz displays

params.description: Research on scroll momentum settling stutter in Electron on macOS ProMotion 120Hz displays revealed multiple root causes: 1) macOS ProMotion VRR drops from 120Hz to 60Hz during momentum scroll deceleration phase as Apple's adaptive refresh rate conserves power when input velocity decreases, 2) Chromium's CVDisplayLink doesn't properly handle VRR transitions causing frame pacing hitches (CADisplayLink fix in progress), 3) Electron's _cornerMask override caused WindowServer GPU spikes on macOS 26 (fixed in Electron 38.2.0+), 4) CSS layout patterns with nested flex + min-height: 0 can create non-fast scrollable regions forcing main-thread scroll fallback

Electron scroll performance on macOS ProMotion 120Hz: the remaining momentum settling stutter is a known Chromium bug (Issue 40202100) — CVDisplayLink doesn't handle VRR refresh rate tier transitions cleanly during scroll deceleration. This is NOT fixable at the app level. Key fixes that DID help: (1) Remove continuous RAF loops (FPS counter was worst offender), (2) Replace Tailwind animate-pulse with compositor-isolated animation (will-change: opacity + contain: strict), (3) Add overscroll-behavior: contain to scroll containers, (4) Flatten flex nesting depth to reduce non-fast scrollable regions, (5) Remove all custom scrollbar CSS, (6) Remove will-change: transform from scroll containers. Flags that made things WORSE: disable-frame-rate-limit and disable-gpu-vsync dropped FPS to 50.

Upgraded Electron from 33.4.11 to 40.6.0 to fix macOS 26 Tahoe WindowServer GPU bug (electron/electron#48311). This was the single biggest performance improvement — went from 20fps feel to near-native.

Both crash reports show identical stack: pty.node Napi::Error::ThrowAsJavaScriptException during node::Environment::CleanupHandles. node-pty tries to throw a JS exception from a ThreadSafeFunction callback during environment shutdown, which calls std::terminate -> abort(). params.goal: Research node-pty SIGABRT crash in Electron during environment cleanup

params.description: node-pty SIGABRT crash in Electron is a known bug with specific fixes. The crash happens when node-pty's ThreadSafeFunction callback tries to throw a JS exception during environment teardown (napi_closing state). PR #672 (merged into v1.1.0) fixed the assertion failure by handling napi_closing status in SetupExitCallback. But the VS Code issue #243952 shows the IDENTICAL crash stack (ThrowAsJavaScriptException -> CallJS -> CleanupHandles) is STILL happening on v1.1.0+ with Electron 34+. The fix in PR #672 handles BlockingCall returning napi_closing, but the crash in #243952 happens inside the CallJS default callback itself when it tries to call a JS function on a torn-down environment. These are two different code paths.

Fixed node-pty SIGABRT crash on app quit. Root cause: before-quit handler called pty.kill() without disposing event listeners first. node-pty's native exit callback thread would fire ThreadSafeFunction::CallJS during environment teardown, throwing an uncatchable C++ exception -> std::terminate -> SIGABRT. Fix: dispose dataDisposable and exitDisposable first, then kill via process.kill(pid, SIGKILL) to bypass node-pty native cleanup, then delay app.quit() by 100ms to let native threads settle.

Always read crash reports before guessing root causes. We spent two sessions assuming the freeze was Claude JSON.parse blocking the main process. The actual crash was node-pty SIGABRT during cleanup — completely unrelated. The crash logs (~/Library/Logs/DiagnosticReports/) had the answer the whole time: pty.node in the stack trace, not anything Claude-related.

Electron UtilityProcess is a truly separate OS process with its own PID and address space. Native .node addons loaded via require() in a UtilityProcess are loaded into that process's address space only — they cannot "leak" into the main process. Communication between main and utility processes is exclusively via Mojo IPC (serialized messages), with zero shared memory for native modules. If crash reports show pty.node on CrBrowserMain thread, the crash is either: (1) misattributed in the crash report (utility process thread 0 can sometimes be labeled CrBrowserMain in symbolication tools), or (2) there's an accidental import path pulling node-pty into the main process bundle. VS Code issue #243952 documents the exact same crash (pty.node Napi::ThreadSafeFunction::CallJS during node::Environment::CleanupHandles) and it occurs in the UTILITY process during shutdown, not the main process.

Fixed all 7 structural quality problems in Pulse. (1) Generator prompt now uses a decision tree — IF hidden gems → MUST open with them, ELSE IF buried strengths → MUST open with those, ELSE sharpest gap. No discretion. (2) Critique now has hard gates (deterministic, no LLM): banned words, word count, must end with question, no sales closes, must reference verifiable audit data, subject line checks. Gates run BEFORE LLM scoring — fail any gate = instant reject. (3) Deep research findings forced into opening via the decision tree. (4) Critique criteria redesigned: 6 criteria focused on impact (opening_hook, diagnosis_depth, voice_authenticity, cost_clarity, close_quality, would_you_reply) with harsh scoring instructions. LLM prompts that say 'consider' or 'if available, lead with' produce inconsistent output. The model treats suggestions as optional. Decision trees (IF X → MUST do Y, ELSE → MUST do Z) produce consistently strong output. Same principle applies to quality gates: deterministic checks (word in banned list? reject) are more reliable than asking an LLM to score how well an email avoids generic language.

Pulse's learning system is a dead loop. Learnings are calculated (industry reply rates, score brackets, timing) and stored in DB, but NOTHING reads them back. The generator doesn't know which industries respond better. The critique doesn't know which opening styles get replies. The brain doesn't grow. The system sends email #100 with the same knowledge it had at email #1.

Three feedback loops: (1) Emails that get replies are auto-promoted to exemplars — the brain literally grows from what works. Proven exemplars get 60% selection priority over handwritten ones. (2) Industry intelligence injected into generator prompt — if tourism responds at 15% but restaurants at 2%, the system writes differently for each, with explicit coaching. (3) Critique calibration from real data — the system tracks which scoring criteria actually correlate with replies and tells the critique LLM to weight those more heavily.

Pulse lacks rich context accumulation. It has a pipeline (scrape → analyze → write) but no world model. No knowledge graph per prospect.
